title: London Transport API - Real-World Project
description: Build a real-time London Underground tracker using TfL's live API data

api_connection_failed.message: рҹ”Ң API connection issues? Let me help! Common fixes: 1) Check your API key, 2) Verify the endpoint URL, 3) Check your internet connection. Want me to walk you through it?

json_parsing_error.message: рҹ“Ҡ JSON parsing error! The API returns nested data. Let me show you how to navigate it step by step.

overwhelmed_by_data.message: рҹҳ… That's a LOT of API data! Let's break it down. Focus on just ONE field first - like 'lineName'. Want me to show you?

successful_api_call.message: рҹҺү YES! You just made your first REAL API call! You're now working with LIVE London Transport data! Ready to parse it?

project_complete.message: рҹҸҶ AMAZING! You built a real-world project! You can now: вң… Make API calls вң… Parse JSON вң… Handle errors вң… Display live data. Add this to your portfolio!

lessons.title: London Transport API - Your First Real API!
lessons.ai_welcome_message: рҹҡҮ Welcome to the REAL WORLD! We're using Transport for London's LIVE API. Every request you make gets ACTUAL data from London's Underground right now. This is professional-level stuff!

theory.introduction: APIs (Application Programming Interfaces) let programs talk to each other. TfL's API gives us real-time data about every tube line, bus, and station in London. We'll build a tracker that shows live arrival times!
theory.why_important: This is how modern apps work! Twitter, Instagram, Google Maps - they ALL use APIs. By mastering this, you can build apps that use ANY web service. This skill gets you hired!

key_concepts.explanation: An API is like a waiter in a restaurant. You (the program) ask the waiter (API) for something, and they bring back data from the kitchen (server). You don't need to know how the kitchen works!
key_concepts.example:
# Making an API request
import requests

response = requests.get('https://api.tfl.gov.uk/Line/victoria/Status')
data = response.json()  # Convert response to Python dictionary

print(data)  # Real-time Victoria Line status!
key_concepts.ai_hint: рҹ’Ў Think of it like this: You're texting TfL and asking 'How's the Victoria Line?' They text back with live data!

key_concepts.explanation: An endpoint is a specific URL that gives you specific data. TfL has different endpoints for lines, arrivals, stations, disruptions, etc.
key_concepts.example:
# Different endpoints for different data

# Get line status
status_url = 'https://api.tfl.gov.uk/Line/victoria/Status'

# Get arrivals at a station
arrivals_url = 'https://api.tfl.gov.uk/StopPoint/940GZZLUKSX/Arrivals'

# Get all tube lines
lines_url = 'https://api.tfl.gov.uk/Line/Mode/tube'
key_concepts.ai_hint: рҹҺҜ Each endpoint is like a different phone number at TfL - one for status, one for arrivals, one for stations!

key_concepts.explanation: APIs return data in JSON (JavaScript Object Notation). Python converts it to dictionaries and lists. You navigate it like nested boxes!
key_concepts.ai_hint: вҡ пёҸ Common mistake: Forgetting that 'lineStatuses' is a LIST []. Use [0] to get the first status!

key_concepts.explanation: When you make an API request, you get a status code. 200 = success, 404 = not found, 500 = server error.
key_concepts.ai_hint: рҹ”Ҙ ALWAYS check status_code before parsing JSON! Otherwise your program crashes!

key_concepts.explanation: APIs can fail (no internet, server down, wrong URL). Use try/except to handle errors gracefully.
key_concepts.ai_hint: рҹ’Ә Professional programmers ALWAYS handle errors. Never assume the API will work!

ai_reading_checkpoints.message: рҹ“– You're learning the theory! Want to see a REAL API call right now? Let me show you LIVE data!
рҹӨ” This is complex stuff! Let's make it real. I'll give you starter code to make your FIRST API call. Ready?

visual_diagram.title: How API Requests Work - The Journey of Data
visual_diagram.content:
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ  YOUR PYTHON PROGRAM                   в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  'Show me Victoria Line status'        в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
                в”Ӯ
                в”Ӯ 1. HTTP GET Request
                вҶ“
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ  TRANSPORT FOR LONDON API SERVER       в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  Receives: GET /Line/victoria/Status   в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
                в”Ӯ
                в”Ӯ 2. Server checks database
                вҶ“
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ  TfL LIVE DATABASE                     в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  Current status: Good Service          в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
                в”Ӯ
                в”Ӯ 3. Returns JSON data
                вҶ“
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ  YOUR PROGRAM                          в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  Receives: {"name": "Victoria", ...}   в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  Displays: Victoria Line - Good Serviceв”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

TIMING:
1. Request sent: ~100ms (0.1 seconds)
2. TfL processes: ~200ms
3. Response received: ~100ms
Total: ~400ms (less than half a second!)

ERROR SCENARIOS:
вқҢ No internet вҶ’ ConnectionError
вқҢ Wrong URL вҶ’ 404 Not Found
вқҢ TfL server down вҶ’ 500 Server Error
вң… Always handle these cases!
visual_diagram.ai_diagram_prompt: рҹ‘Җ Looking at the flow? Want me to show you this in action with REAL CODE?

complete_examples.title: Example 1: Get Victoria Line Status (Simplest API Call)
complete_examples.description: Your first real API call - get live status of the Victoria Line
complete_examples.code:
import requests

# TfL API endpoint for Victoria Line status
url = 'https://api.tfl.gov.uk/Line/victoria/Status'

# Make the request
response = requests.get(url)

# Check if successful
if response.status_code == 200:
    data = response.json()
    
    # Extract line name and status
    line_name = data[0]['name']
    status = data[0]['lineStatuses'][0]['statusSeverityDescription']
    
    # Display result
    print('=' * 50)
    print(f'рҹҡҮ Line: {line_name}')
    print(f'рҹ“Ҡ Status: {status}')
    print('=' * 50)
else:
    print(f'вқҢ Error: Status code {response.status_code}')
complete_examples.ai_explanation: рҹҺҜ See how we used [0] twice? That's because TfL returns a LIST of lines (even for one line), and lineStatuses is also a LIST. Navigate carefully!
complete_examples.ai_challenge: рҹ”Ҙ Challenge: Change 'victoria' to 'central' in the URL and run it again!

complete_examples.title: Example 2: Check Multiple Lines at Once
complete_examples.description: Get status for all major tube lines in one API call
complete_examples.code:
import requests

# Get ALL tube lines status
url = 'https://api.tfl.gov.uk/Line/Mode/tube/Status'

try:
    response = requests.get(url, timeout=5)
    response.raise_for_status()
    
    lines = response.json()
    
    print('рҹҡҮ LONDON UNDERGROUND STATUS')
    print('=' * 60)
    
    for line in lines:
        name = line['name']
        status = line['lineStatuses'][0]['statusSeverityDescription']
        
        # Add emoji based on status
        if 'Good Service' in status:
            emoji = 'вң…'
        elif 'Minor Delays' in status:
            emoji = 'вҡ пёҸ'
        else:
            emoji = 'вқҢ'
        
        print(f'{emoji} {name:20} | {status}')
    
    print('=' * 60)
    
except requests.exceptions.RequestException as e:
    print(f'вқҢ API Error: {e}')
complete_examples.output:
рҹҡҮ LONDON UNDERGROUND STATUS
============================================================
вң… Bakerloo              | Good Service
вң… Central               | Good Service
вң… Circle                | Good Service
вҡ пёҸ District              | Minor Delays
вң… Hammersmith & City    | Good Service
вң… Jubilee               | Good Service
вқҢ Metropolitan          | Severe Delays
вң… Northern              | Good Service
вң… Piccadilly            | Good Service
вң… Victoria              | Good Service
вң… Waterloo & City       | Good Service
============================================================
complete_examples.ai_explanation: рҹ’Ә Now we're talking! This is a REAL dashboard. We loop through all lines and add emojis for visual feedback. Professional stuff!
complete_examples.ai_challenge: рҹҺҜ Challenge: Count how many lines have 'Good Service' and print the total at the end!

complete_examples.title: Example 3: Get Live Arrivals at King's Cross
complete_examples.description: See real-time train arrivals at King's Cross St. Pancras station
complete_examples.code:
import requests
from datetime import datetime

# King's Cross St. Pancras station ID
station_id = '940GZZLUKSX'
url = f'https://api.tfl.gov.uk/StopPoint/{station_id}/Arrivals'

try:
    response = requests.get(url, timeout=5)
    response.raise_for_status()
    
    arrivals = response.json()
    
    # Sort by time to station (closest first)
    arrivals.sort(key=lambda x: x['timeToStation'])
    
    print('рҹҡҮ KING\'S CROSS ST. PANCRAS - LIVE ARRIVALS')
    print('=' * 70)
    print(f'рҹ“… Updated: {datetime.now().strftime("%H:%M:%S")}')
    print('=' * 70)
    
    # Show next 10 trains
    for arrival in arrivals[:10]:
        line = arrival['lineName']
        destination = arrival['destinationName']
        minutes = arrival['timeToStation'] // 60
        platform = arrival.get('platformName', 'N/A')
        
        if minutes == 0:
            time_str = 'Due'
        elif minutes == 1:
            time_str = '1 min'
        else:
            time_str = f'{minutes} mins'
        
        print(f'рҹҡҶ {line:20} вҶ’ {destination:25} | {time_str:8} | {platform}')
    
    print('=' * 70)
    
except requests.exceptions.RequestException as e:
    print(f'вқҢ API Error: {e}')
complete_examples.output:
рҹҡҮ KING'S CROSS ST. PANCRAS - LIVE ARRIVALS
======================================================================
рҹ“… Updated: 14:35:22
======================================================================
рҹҡҶ Victoria              вҶ’ Brixton                  | Due      | Platform 3
рҹҡҶ Northern              вҶ’ Morden                   | 2 mins   | Platform 1
рҹҡҶ Piccadilly            вҶ’ Heathrow Terminal 5      | 3 mins   | Platform 4
рҹҡҶ Victoria              вҶ’ Walthamstow Central      | 4 mins   | Platform 4
рҹҡҶ Northern              вҶ’ High Barnet              | 5 mins   | Platform 2
рҹҡҶ Piccadilly            вҶ’ Cockfosters              | 6 mins   | Platform 3
рҹҡҶ Victoria              вҶ’ Brixton                  | 7 mins   | Platform 3
рҹҡҶ Northern              вҶ’ Edgware                  | 8 mins   | Platform 1
рҹҡҶ Piccadilly            вҶ’ Heathrow Terminal 4      | 9 mins   | Platform 4
рҹҡҶ Victoria              вҶ’ Walthamstow Central      | 10 mins  | Platform 4
======================================================================
complete_examples.ai_explanation: рҹ”Ҙ THIS IS LIVE DATA! Every time you run this, you get REAL train times. Professional apps like Citymapper do exactly this!

complete_examples.title: Example 4: Find Nearest Station with Coordinates
complete_examples.description: Use latitude/longitude to find the closest tube station
complete_examples.code:
import requests

# Coordinates for Buckingham Palace
lat = 51.5014
lon = -0.1419

url = f'https://api.tfl.gov.uk/StopPoint/?lat={lat}&lon={lon}&stopTypes=NaptanMetroStation&radius=500'

try:
    response = requests.get(url, timeout=5)
    response.raise_for_status()
    
    stations = response.json()['stopPoints']
    
    print('рҹ—әпёҸ  NEAREST TUBE STATIONS')
    print(f'рҹ“Қ Location: {lat}, {lon}')
    print('=' * 60)
    
    for station in stations[:5]:
        name = station['commonName']
        distance = station['distance']
        lines = ', '.join([line['name'] for line in station['lines']])
        
        print(f'рҹҡҮ {name}')
        print(f'   рҹ“Ҹ Distance: {distance:.0f} meters')
        print(f'   рҹҡҶ Lines: {lines}')
        print()
    
except requests.exceptions.RequestException as e:
    print(f'вқҢ API Error: {e}')
complete_examples.output:
рҹ—әпёҸ  NEAREST TUBE STATIONS
рҹ“Қ Location: 51.5014, -0.1419
============================================================
рҹҡҮ St. James's Park Underground Station
   рҹ“Ҹ Distance: 423 meters
   рҹҡҶ Lines: District, Circle

рҹҡҮ Victoria Underground Station
   пҝҪпҝҪ Distance: 487 meters
   рҹҡҶ Lines: Victoria, District, Circle

рҹҡҮ Westminster Underground Station
   рҹ“Ҹ Distance: 612 meters
   рҹҡҶ Lines: Jubilee, District, Circle

рҹҡҮ Green Park Underground Station
   рҹ“Ҹ Distance: 785 meters
   рҹҡҶ Lines: Piccadilly, Victoria, Jubilee

рҹҡҮ Hyde Park Corner Underground Station
   рҹ“Ҹ Distance: 892 meters
   рҹҡҶ Lines: Piccadilly
complete_examples.ai_explanation: рҹҢҚ Location-based queries! This is how maps apps work. You give coordinates, the API finds nearby stations. GPS magic!
complete_examples.ai_challenge: рҹҺҜ Challenge: Try coordinates for your own location! Use Google Maps to get your lat/lon.

complete_examples.title: Example 5: Journey Planner
complete_examples.description: Plan a journey between two stations with live data
complete_examples.code:
import requests
from datetime import datetime

# Journey: King's Cross to Heathrow Airport
from_station = '940GZZLUKSX'  # King's Cross
to_station = '940GZZLULHR5'  # Heathrow Terminal 5

url = f'https://api.tfl.gov.uk/Journey/JourneyResults/{from_station}/to/{to_station}'

try:
    response = requests.get(url, timeout=10)
    response.raise_for_status()
    
    data = response.json()
    journeys = data['journeys']
    
    print('рҹ—әпёҸ  JOURNEY PLANNER')
    print('=' * 70)
    print(f'рҹ“Қ From: King\'s Cross St. Pancras')
    print(f'рҹ“Қ To: Heathrow Terminal 5')
    print(f'рҹ“… Time: {datetime.now().strftime("%H:%M")}')
    print('=' * 70)
    
    for i, journey in enumerate(journeys[:3], 1):
        duration = journey['duration']
        print(f'\nрҹҡҶ OPTION {i}')
        print(f'вҸұпёҸ  Duration: {duration} minutes')
        print(f'рҹ“Ҡ Steps:')
        
        for leg in journey['legs']:
            mode = leg['mode']['name']
            
            if leg['mode']['id'] == 'walking':
                duration_leg = leg['duration']
                print(f'   рҹҡ¶ Walk {duration_leg} mins')
            else:
                line = leg.get('routeOptions', [{}])[0].get('name', 'Unknown')
                from_point = leg['departurePoint']['commonName']
                to_point = leg['arrivalPoint']['commonName']
                duration_leg = leg['duration']
                
                print(f'   рҹҡҮ {line}')
                print(f'      From: {from_point}')
                print(f'      To: {to_point}')
                print(f'      Duration: {duration_leg} mins')
    
    print('\n' + '=' * 70)
    
except requests.exceptions.RequestException as e:
    print(f'вқҢ API Error: {e}')
except KeyError as e:
    print(f'вҡ пёҸ  Data parsing error: {e}')
complete_examples.output:

complete_examples.ai_explanation: рҹҡҖ PROFESSIONAL LEVEL! You just built a journey planner like Google Maps! This shows multiple route options with real-time data. Portfolio-worthy!

practice_exercises.title: Station Arrival Board
practice_exercises.description: Build a live arrival board for any station showing next 5 trains
practice_exercises.ai_intro: рҹҡҶ Now you're building a REAL station board! Like the ones at every tube platform. This is pro-level!
practice_exercises.starter_code:
import requests

# Common station IDs:
# King's Cross: 940GZZLUKSX
# Liverpool Street: 940GZZLULVT
# Oxford Circus: 940GZZLUOXC
# Waterloo: 940GZZLUWLO

station_id = input('Enter station ID: ')
url = f'https://api.tfl.gov.uk/StopPoint/{station_id}/Arrivals'

try:
    response = requests.get(url, timeout=5)
    
    if response.status_code == 200:
        arrivals = response.json()
        
        # TODO: Sort arrivals by timeToStation (closest first)
        # Hint: arrivals.sort(key=lambda x: x['timeToStation'])
        
        print('рҹҡҮ LIVE ARRIVALS')
        print('=' * 60)
        
        # TODO: Loop through first 5 arrivals
        for arrival in arrivals[:5]:
            # TODO: Extract: lineName, destinationName, timeToStation
            line = # Your code
            destination = # Your code
            seconds = # Your code
            
            # TODO: Convert seconds to minutes
            minutes = # Your code
            
            # TODO: Show 'Due' if 0 mins, otherwise show minutes
            if minutes == 0:
                time_str = 'Due'
            else:
                time_str = f'{minutes} min'
            
            # TODO: Print formatted result
            print(f'рҹҡҶ {line:20} вҶ’ {destination:25} | {time_str}')
        
        print('=' * 60)
    else:
        print(f'вқҢ Error: {response.status_code}')
        
except Exception as e:
    print(f'вҡ пёҸ  Error: {e}')

hints.message:
рҹ“Ҡ To sort arrivals:
   arrivals.sort(key=lambda x: x['timeToStation'])
   This puts closest trains first!

рҹҺҜ To extract data:
   line = arrival['lineName']
   destination = arrival['destinationName']
   seconds = arrival['timeToStation']

вҸұпёҸ  To convert to minutes:
   minutes = seconds // 60
   Use // for whole number division!

practice_exercises.solution:
import requests

station_id = input('Enter station ID: ')
url = f'https://api.tfl.gov.uk/StopPoint/{station_id}/Arrivals'

try:
    response = requests.get(url, timeout=5)
    
    if response.status_code == 200:
        arrivals = response.json()
        arrivals.sort(key=lambda x: x['timeToStation'])
        
        print('рҹҡҮ LIVE ARRIVALS')
        print('=' * 60)
        
        for arrival in arrivals[:5]:
            line = arrival['lineName']
            destination = arrival['destinationName']
            seconds = arrival['timeToStation']
            minutes = seconds // 60
            
            if minutes == 0:
                time_str = 'Due'
            else:
                time_str = f'{minutes} min'
            
            print(f'рҹҡҶ {line:20} вҶ’ {destination:25} | {time_str}')
        
        print('=' * 60)
    else:
        print(f'вқҢ Error: {response.status_code}')
        
except requests.exceptions.RequestException as e:
    print(f'вҡ пёҸ  API Error: {e}')

practice_exercises.title: Disruption Alert System
practice_exercises.description: Build a system that alerts you of any disruptions on your favorite lines
practice_exercises.ai_intro: рҹҡЁ Time to build a MONITORING SYSTEM! This checks multiple lines and alerts you to problems. Real DevOps stuff!
practice_exercises.starter_code:
import requests
import time
from datetime import datetime

# Your favorite lines
favorite_lines = ['victoria', 'northern', 'central']

def check_line_status(line_name):
    """Check status of a single line"""
    url = f'https://api.tfl.gov.uk/Line/{line_name}/Status'
    
    try:
        response = requests.get(url, timeout=5)
        
        if response.status_code == 200:
            data = response.json()
            # TODO: Extract status
            status = # Your code
            return status
        else:
            return None
            
    except Exception as e:
        return None

def check_all_lines():
    """Check all favorite lines"""
    print(f'\nрҹ”Қ Checking lines at {datetime.now().strftime("%H:%M:%S")}')
    print('=' * 60)
    
    disruptions = []
    
    # TODO: Loop through favorite_lines
    for line in favorite_lines:
        status = check_line_status(line)
        
        if status:
            # TODO: Check if status contains problems
            # If 'Good Service' вҶ’ вң…
            # Otherwise вҶ’ вҡ пёҸ and add to disruptions list
            
            if 'Good Service' in status:
                print(f'вң… {line.title():20} | {status}')
            else:
                print(f'вҡ пёҸ {line.title():20} | {status}')
                # TODO: Add to disruptions list
                disruptions.append(# Your code)
        else:
            print(f'вқҢ {line.title():20} | Could not check')
    
    print('=' * 60)
    
    # TODO: If there are disruptions, show alert
    if len(disruptions) > 0:
        print(f'\nрҹҡЁ ALERT: {len(disruptions)} line(s) with disruptions!')
        for line, status in disruptions:
            print(f'   вҡ пёҸ  {line}: {status}')
    else:
        print('\nвң… All lines running smoothly!')
    
    return disruptions

# TODO: Run check every 60 seconds
print('рҹҡҮ TfL DISRUPTION MONITOR')
print('Monitoring:', ', '.join([l.title() for l in favorite_lines]))
print('Press Ctrl+C to stop')

try:
    while True:
        check_all_lines()
        # TODO: Wait 60 seconds before next check
        time.sleep(# Your code)
except KeyboardInterrupt:
    print('\n\nрҹ‘Ӣ Monitor stopped!')

hints.message:
рҹҺҜ To add disruption:
   disruptions.append((line, status))
   This creates a tuple with line name and status!

вҸұпёҸ  Full solution structure:

1. Extract status in check_line_status()
2. In check_all_lines(), check if 'Good Service' in status
3. If not, append (line, status) to disruptions
4. Use time.sleep(60) to wait
